sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 5.2g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a legume, fibre and practicality are supposed to carry the case.
sections.subtitleText: 5.2g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a legume, fibre and practicality are supposed to carry the case.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 8.9g of carbs. glycemic index, fibre, and sugar are doing most of the work. for legumes, the carbs look much better when fibre and protein are both backing them up.
sections.subtitleText: 8.9g of carbs. glycemic index, fibre, and sugar are doing most of the work. for legumes, the carbs look much better when fibre and protein are both backing them up.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 11.9g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein support is a big part of what makes legumes worth it.
sections.subtitleText: 11.9g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein support is a big part of what makes legumes worth it.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin c at 10% daily value stands out most. that is barely moving the needle.
sections.subtitleText: vitamin c at 10% daily value stands out most. that is barely moving the needle.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: iron at 15% daily value, magnesium at 16% daily value, zinc at 12% daily value stand out most. useful, but still pretty limited.
sections.subtitleText: iron at 15% daily value, magnesium at 16% daily value, zinc at 12% daily value stand out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: pros first: one of the cheaper ways to buy useful nutrition. that matters for real diets, not just theory. filling for the calories. legumes usually earn a lot of practical value from satiety. cheap staple with real payoff. this matters because a food is stronger when people can actually use it often.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: now the downsides: can be rough on digestion for some people. practical tolerance still matters. can take planning or prep. that is a small but real friction cost. prep time can still be a barrier. dry legumes especially ask more from the cook.

closing.summary: Edamame does have some solid upside. but the tradeoffs keep it from climbing higher. best when you want fibre, protein, and actual meal utility together.
